theory.intro: **Frequency** is how many times a result actually happened in an experiment. **Probability** is the expected chance of that result, worked out from equally likely outcomes. They are not the same thing.
facts: **Frequency** is a count from real trials (it is a whole number).
**Probability** is a fraction predicting the chance of an outcome before you experiment.
**Relative frequency** $= \dfrac{\text{frequency of result}}{\text{total trials}}$ — this can be compared with the probability.
steps: Decide whether the value comes from counting trials (frequency) or from the structure of the experiment (probability).
To compare them, turn a frequency into a relative frequency: count over total trials.

foundation.q: A coin is tossed $20$ times and lands heads $11$ times. Is $11$ a frequency or a probability?
For a fair coin, $P(\text{heads}) = \tfrac{1}{2}$. Is $\tfrac{1}{2}$ a frequency or a probability?
A die is rolled $30$ times; a $4$ comes up $6$ times. State the frequency of $4$.
A die is rolled $30$ times; a $4$ comes up $6$ times. State the relative frequency of $4$.
What is the probability of rolling a $4$ on a fair die?
In the table, what is the frequency of blue? $\begin{array}{c|c} \text{red} & 7 \\ \hline \text{blue} & 5 \end{array}$
A spinner is spun $12$ times and lands on green $3$ times. Find the relative frequency of green.
True or false: probability is found by counting how many times something happened.
A coin lands tails $9$ times out of $20$. Find the relative frequency of tails.
The chance of drawing a red card from a fair set of $4$ colours is $\tfrac{1}{4}$. Frequency or probability?

development.q: Results: $\begin{array}{c|c|c|c} \text{red} & \text{blue} & \text{green} & \text{total} \\ \hline 8 & 5 & 7 & 20 \end{array}$ Find the relative frequency of red.
A fair $4$-colour spinner is spun $40$ times. State the probability of red and the expected frequency of red.
A die rolled $60$ times gave a $2$ exactly $9$ times. Compare the relative frequency with the probability of $2$.
A coin tossed $50$ times landed heads $23$ times. State the frequency and the relative frequency of heads.
Why might the relative frequency of heads not equal $\tfrac{1}{2}$ after $20$ tosses?
development.a: random variation in a small number of trials

development.q: Results table: $\begin{array}{c|c} \text{outcome} & \text{freq} \\ \hline 1 & 4 \\ 2 & 6 \\ 3 & 5 \end{array}$ How many trials in total?
From the table above, find the relative frequency of outcome $2$.
A spinner has $5$ equal sectors. State $P(\text{any one sector})$ and the expected frequency over $100$ spins.
A bag draw gave red $12$ times in $30$ draws. Does the relative frequency $\tfrac{2}{5}$ tell you the exact probability?
development.a: no — it is only an estimate

development.q: A die rolled $24$ times gave even numbers $14$ times. Compare the relative frequency of even with $P(\text{even})$.

mastery.q: A die rolled $200$ times gave a $6$ exactly $30$ times. Is the relative frequency above or below $P(6)$?
A spinner gave: red $18$, blue $22$ in $40$ spins. If the spinner is fair with $2$ colours, do the results match the probability well?
A coin gave $46$ heads in $100$ tosses. How many more heads would give a relative frequency of exactly $\tfrac{1}{2}$ (same total)?
A loaded die rolled $50$ times gave a $1$ exactly $20$ times. Does this look fair? Explain.
A spinner has $P(\text{red}) = \tfrac{1}{4}$. In $80$ spins, red appeared $25$ times. Find the difference between actual and expected frequency.
After $10$ tosses a coin shows $7$ heads; after $1000$ tosses it shows $503$. Which relative frequency is closer to $\tfrac{1}{2}$?
A bag draw over $25$ trials gave green $10$ times. A student says $P(\text{green}) = \tfrac{2}{5}$ exactly. Why might they be wrong?
mastery.a: it is an estimate, not the true probability

mastery.q: A die rolled $120$ times: even $66$, odd $54$. Find the relative frequency of even and compare with $P(\text{even})$.
A coin's relative frequency of heads is $\tfrac{3}{5}$ after $n$ tosses, with $30$ heads. Find $n$.
